source_note: Wiktionary case Etym1 'from Latin casus (a falling, a fall; accident, event)' vs Etym2 'from Latin capsa (box, bookcase)'; Etymonline separate headwords case(n.1) casus / case(n.2) capsa. Two distinct common noun origins.

key_roots: PGmc *balluz 'round object' | OFr bal / LL ballare 'to dance'
source_note: Wiktionary ball Etym1 'Old Norse bǫllr ... Proto-Germanic *balluz' vs Etym2 'Old French bal (dance), from Late Latin ballāre (to dance)'. Two distinct origins, both common nouns (round object vs formal dance).

key_roots: Gk daktulos 'finger' (the fruit) | L datus 'given' (calendar date)
source_note: Wiktionary date Etym1 'Latin dactylus ... Ancient Greek daktulos (finger)' (the fruit) vs Etym2 'Late Latin data, from Latin datus (given)' (point in time). Two distinct common noun origins.

Wiktionary school Etym1 'Old English scōl (place of education)' vs Etym2 '...Middle English schole (group of persons, host, company)' = school of fish (MDu schole). Distinct origins; standard references treat the fish sense as a separate origin (Wiktionary phrases Etym2 as 'influenced by' — nuance noted).

Etymonline bank(n.1) 'from Old Italian banca and also from French banque' (financial) vs bank(n.2) 'natural earthen incline ... from a Scandinavian source ... Old Norse bakki (river bank, ridge, mound)'. Two distinct common noun origins.

key_roots: L planum 'flat surface' | LL plana 'planing tool' | clipping of aeroplane
source_note: Wiktionary plane Etym1 'Latin plānum (flat surface)' vs Etym2 'Late Latin plāna (planing tool)' vs Etym3 'Clipping of aeroplane'. Multiple distinct noun origins (the Platanus tree sense unconfirmed in this fetch, not relied on).

key_roots: OE teran 'to rip' (a rip) | OE tēar 'tear (eye-drop)'
source_note: Wiktionary tear Etym1 'Old English teran (to tear, lacerate)' (PIE der-) vs Etym2 'Old English teagor, tēar' (eye-drop, PIE dakru-). Two distinct OE origins; the rip-noun derives from the verb, eye-drop is primarily a noun.

key_roots: OE seolh (the marine mammal) | L sigillum 'stamp/impression'
source_note: Wiktionary seal Etym1 'Old English seolh ... Proto-Germanic *selhaz' (animal) vs Etym2 'Anglo-Norman seel, from Latin sigillum (diminutive of signum, sign)' (stamp). Two distinct common noun origins.

Wiktionary pitch Etym1 'Old English piċ ... from Latin pix' (tar/resin) vs Etym2 'Old English *piċċan (to thrust in, fasten, settle)' (throw); Etymonline confirms pitch(n.2) resin / pitch(v.1) thrust. Field, throw, and musical-pitch nouns descend from the thrust lineage — distinct from tar.
Wiktionary pitcher Etym1 'From pitch + -er' (agent noun) vs Etym2 'Old French pichier (small jug) ... Late/Medieval Latin pīcārium' (vessel). Two distinct common noun origins.

key_roots: OE *rocc 'stone' | OE roccian 'to sway' (> rock music genre)
source_note: Wiktionary rock Etym1 'Old English *rocc (rock)' (stone) vs Etym2 'Old English roccian (to rock, sway)' and Etym3 'From rock and roll, shortened' (music genre derives from the sway verb). Stone-noun vs music-noun are etymologically distinct. Nuance: music descends from sway.
Single Old English origin (OE bodig); all senses (physical body / main part / group / corpse) are related extensions.
Single origin (Latin vox); vocal-sound / opinion / grammatical-voice / singer senses are related extensions.
Single Old English origin (OE mūþ); body-part / opening / river-mouth / spokesperson senses are related extensions.
Single Old English origin (OE heorte); organ / emotional-centre / core / courage / card-suit senses are related extensions.
Single Latin origin (granum); seed / cereal / texture / small-amount senses are related extensions (v2 stratification confirmed POLYSEME).
Single Old English origin (OE fȳr); combustion / gunfire / passion / dismissal senses are related extensions.
Single origin (Old French cort, from Latin cohors/court-yard); royal-court / law-court / sports-court / courtship senses are related extensions.
Single Old English origin (OE bedd); sleeping-furniture / garden-bed / river-bed / layer senses are related extensions.
Single Old English origin (OE drincan); beverage / alcoholic-drink / act-of-drinking senses are related extensions.
Single PIE root *steig- (OE sticca 'rod' and stician 'pierce/adhere' are one lineage); rod / control-lever / adhesion senses are related.
Single origin (Latin trahere via Old French train); railway-train / sequence / retinue / dress-train / to-train senses are all related extensions of 'pull/draw along'.

key_roots: OE græf 'burial pit' | L gravis 'heavy/serious' (mark) | ME greyve (steward)
source_note: AMBIGUOUS -> EXCLUDED. Dominant common noun (burial place, OE græf) is single-origin; the competing noun senses (grave-accent mark from L gravis; steward from greyve) are technical/archaic, NOT common general-English nouns. Fails the gate's 'second origin common in modern English' condition.

key_roots: OE cǣġ 'lock/musical/explanatory key' | Sp cayo 'small island/reef'
source_note: AMBIGUOUS -> EXCLUDED. Two genuinely distinct origins (OE cǣġ vs Spanish cayo, per Etymonline key(n.1)/key(n.2)), but the island sense (also 'cay') is regional/geographic (Florida Keys), not high-frequency general English; WiC 'key' items are almost certainly the lock/music/explanation polysemy. Excluded to avoid mislabeling.

key_roots: OE lēoht 'brightness' (PIE *lewk-) [the 'not heavy' root *legwh- is adj/verb]
source_note: INSTRUCTIVE TRAP -> EXCLUDED. WordNet shows 2 noun clusters so the structural rule flags light as a homonym, but Wiktionary+Etymonline confirm the NOUN light is SINGLE-origin (illumination, *lewk-); the etymologically-distinct 'not heavy' root (*legwh-) is adjective/verb, not a noun. So noun-light is a POLYSEME, not a homonym — the false positive that proves the WordNet-structural rule cannot stand alone (the gate's etymology AND-condition is load-bearing). Recorded but excluded from selection.
